op.name: logical_and
op.summary: Compute the truth value of x1 AND x2 element-wise.
op.signature: we.logical_and(x1, x2, /, out=None, *, where=True, casting='same_kind', order='K', dtype=None, subok=True[, signature])

blocks.text: Compute the truth value of x1 AND x2 element-wise.

desc_blocks.text:
Input arrays.
If ``x1.shape != x2.shape``, they must be broadcastable to a common
shape (which becomes the shape of the output).

items.name: out
items.type: ndarray, None, or tuple of ndarray and None, optional

desc_blocks.text:
A location into which the result is stored. If provided, it must have
a shape that the inputs broadcast to. If not provided or None,
a freshly-allocated array is returned. A tuple (possible only as a
keyword argument) must have length equal to the number of outputs.

This condition is broadcast over the input. At locations where the
condition is True, the `out` array will be set to the ufunc result.
Elsewhere, the `out` array will retain its original value.
Note that if an uninitialized `out` array is created via the default
``out=None``, locations within it where the condition is False will
remain uninitialized.

For other keyword-only arguments, see the
:ref:`ufunc docs <ufuncs.kwargs>`.

Boolean result of the logical AND operation applied to the elements
of `x1` and `x2`; the shape is determined by broadcasting.
This is a scalar if both `x1` and `x2` are scalars.

blocks.code:
>>> import numpy as np
>>> np.logical_and(True, False)
False
>>> np.logical_and([True, False], [False, False])
array([False, False])

>>> x = np.arange(5)
>>> np.logical_and(x>1, x<4)
array([False, False,  True,  True, False])

blocks.text:
The ``&`` operator can be used as a shorthand for ``np.logical_and`` on
boolean ndarrays.

blocks.code:
>>> a = np.array([True, False])
>>> b = np.array([False, False])
>>> a & b
array([False, False])
